Summary: In non-small cell lung cancer, PD-L1 as part of the PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint has been identified as a therapeutic target. PD-L1 expression is the preferred biomarker for predicting efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors in advanced NSCLC and high PD-L1 positivity is often accompanied by high levels of T lymphocyte infiltration. Detection of PD-L1 in tumor tissues is the most direct method to predict response to PD‑1/PD‑L1 inhibitors. High PD-L1 expression is associated with greater benefit from single‑agent anti‑PD‑1/PD‑L1 drugs. PD-L1 expression and tumor mutational burden are biomarkers linked to tumor response to combined immune checkpoint inhibitors in NSCLC. In NSCLC patients with high TMB (≥10 mutations/Mb), first‑line nivolumab plus ipilimumab prolonged progression‑free survival regardless of PD‑L1 expression status. The combination of PD‑L1 expression and TMB is considered a promising biomarker for assessing patient survival and response to precise immunotherapy in metastatic NSCLC.
Reference: Tang, S., Qin, C., Hu, H., Liu, T., He, Y., Guo, H., Yan, H., Zhang, J., Tang, S., & Zhou, H. (2022). Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: Progress, Challenges, and Prospects. Cells, 11(3), 320. https://doi.org/10.3390/cells11030320

Summary: PD-L1 is expressed in human NSCLC and the PD-1/PD-L1 axis is targeted by immunotherapies. Tumor cell-surface PD-L1 by immunohistochemistry is used as a predictive biomarker for anti-PD-1/PD-L1 ICIs and four such ICIs are FDA-approved. In EGFR-driven NSCLC cells with exon 19 deletion or L858R mutations EGFR signaling regulates PD-L1. EGFR activation induced PD-L1 via p-ERK1/2/p-c-Jun and via IL-6/JAK/STAT3. PD-L1 induction did not occur via p-AKT/p-S6. EGFR-TKIs or genomic EGFR knockout suppressed anti-CTLA4 antibody–induced PD-L1 expression in NSCLC cells. One study reported that EGFR inhibition reversed T cell apoptosis and enhanced interferon-γ production. In NSCLC YAP regulates PD-L1 transcription via an enhancer with putative TEAD binding sites. Nuclear YAP staining associated with PD-L1 positivity. YAP knockdown decreased PD-L1. YAP overexpression increased PD-L1. Gefitinib-resistant PC9 cells showed increased YAP and PD-L1 and YAP knockdown reduced PD-L1 in resistant cells.
Reference: Hsu, P. C., Jablons, D. M., Yang, C. T., & You, L. (2019). Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor (EGFR) Pathway, Yes-Associated Protein (YAP) and the Regulation of Programmed Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1) in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC). Int J Mol Sci, 20(15), E3821. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijms20153821

Summary: In NSCLC tumor cells, PD-L1 increases expression of cytokines and chemokines including IL6, TNF, TGFB1, IL1B, CXCL1, CXCL3 and CXCL8. In primary lung cancer cell cultures, cytokine and chemokine levels were higher in PD-L1‑high (TPS≥50) tumors than in PD-L1‑low (TPS<1) tumors and levels rose with PD-L1 overexpression and fell with PD-L1 knockdown. PD-L1 overexpression increased IL‑6, phosphorylated Jak2 and Stat3, and nuclear p‑Stat3, and the cytokine increase was blocked by Jak2 and Stat3 inhibitors. In patients with NSCLC PD-L1 status modulated the relationship between IL‑6/Jak/Stat3 pathway enrichment and response to immune checkpoint inhibitors. The pathway was enriched in tumors from ICI responders with PD-L1‑low NSCLC and enriched in ICI non‑responders with PD-L1‑high NSCLC. High IL‑6 expression associated with poor clinical outcomes after ICI therapy in patients with PD-L1‑high NSCLC but not in those with PD-L1‑low NSCLC. Only approximately 40% of patients with PD-L1‑positive NSCLC benefit from ICI therapy, indicating a need to understand resistance mechanisms in PD-L1‑positive NSCLC.
Reference: Jeong, H., Koh, J., Kim, S., Yim, J., Song, S. G., Kim, H., Li, Y., Lee, S. H., Chung, Y. K., Kim, H., Lee, C. H., Kim, H. Y., Keam, B., Lee, S. H., Chung, D. H., & Jeon, Y. K. (2025). Cell-intrinsic PD-L1 signaling drives immunosuppression by myeloid-derived suppressor cells through IL-6/Jak/Stat3 in PD-L1-high lung cancer. J Immunother Cancer, 13(3), e010612. https://doi.org/10.1136/jitc-2024-010612

Summary: The study found that PD‑L1 expression impacted clinical outcomes from immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) therapy in oncogene‑addicted non‑small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). BRAF‑mutant NSCLC exhibited high tumor mutational burden (TMB) and high PD‑L1 expression. The high TMB and high PD‑L1 expression in BRAF‑mutant NSCLC may increase sensitivity to ICB and were associated with superior benefit from ICB. EGFR and HER2 mutations define NSCLC subsets with minimal benefit from ICB. The findings indicate a TMB/PD‑L1‑independent impact of certain oncogene alterations on sensitivity to ICB. PD‑L1 data were available for 4,017 NSCLC patients in the cohort; of the 4,189 patients in the cohort, 4,017 had oncogene alterations and available TMB and PD‑L1 data. Outcomes from ICB monotherapy were assessed in two registry cohorts comprising 1,066 NSCLC patients, but the unavailability of both TMB and PD‑L1 data across the four cohorts precludes more definitive conclusions.
Reference: Negrao, M. V., Skoulidis, F., Montesion, M., Schulze, K., Bara, I., Shen, V., Xu, H., Hu, S., Sui, D., Elamin, Y. Y., Le, X., Goldberg, M. E., Murugesan, K., Wu, C. J., Zhang, J., Barreto, D. S., Robichaux, J. P., Reuben, A., Cascone, T., Gay, C. M., Mitchell, K. G., Hong, L., Rinsurongkawong, W., Roth, J. A., Swisher, S. G., Lee, J., Tsao, A., Papadimitrakopoulou, V., Gibbons, D. L., Glisson, B. S., Singal, G., Miller, V. A., Alexander, B., Frampton, G., Albacker, L. A., Shames, D., Zhang, J., & Heymach, J. V. (2021). Oncogene-specific differences in tumor mutational burden, PD-L1 expression, and outcomes from immunotherapy in non-small cell lung cancer. J Immunother Cancer, 9(8), e002891. https://doi.org/10.1136/jitc-2021-002891

Summary: PD-L1 interacts with the PD-1 receptor on T cells' surface. PD-L1 suppresses the anti-tumor functions of CD8+ T cells. HRS, an ESCRT component, co-localizes with and interacts with PD-L1 in NSCLC cells. HRS mediates sorting of PD-L1 into exosomes and triggers release of exosomal PD-L1. LAMTOR1 resides in late endosomes and lysosomes and associates with HRS and PD-L1. LAMTOR1 suppresses HRS-mediated secretion of exosomal PD-L1 in NSCLC cells. Deletion of HRS markedly diminishes the LAMTOR1–PD-L1 interaction. LAMTOR1 negatively correlates with PD-L1 in 85 NSCLC tissues and regulates the lysosomal membrane and extracellular exosomes, and inhibits the release of exosomal PD-L1 in NSCLC cells. Exosomal PD-L1 restricts CD8+ T cell infiltration in the tumor microenvironment. Suppression of exosomal PD-L1 by LAMTOR1 improves CD8+ T cell function, shown by increased %CFSE− cells, elevated granzyme B, and greater IFN-γ, IL-2, and TNF-α secretion. NSCLC lines H1975 and H358 show notably increased exosomal PD-L1 release. PD-L1 inhibitors yield significant clinical benefits in treating NSCLC and current antibody agents inhibit PD-L1 on cancer cell membranes.
Reference: Wu, B., Huang, X., Shi, X., Jiang, M., Liu, H., & Zhao, L. (2024). LAMTOR1 decreased exosomal PD-L1 to enhance immunotherapy efficacy in non-small cell lung cancer. Mol Cancer, 23(1), 184. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12943-024-02099-4
